steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence has two independent clauses that need to be properly joined: (1) "This conceptualization of harmonic opposites did not originate with [Collier, though]" and (2) "in 1985, the idea was first articulated in Swiss musicologist Ernst Levy's posthumously published book *A Theory of Harmony*." A comma alone between two independent clauses creates a comma splice — the blank must fix this.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Collier, though: Produces "...did not originate with Collier, though in 1985, the idea was first articulated..." — this makes "though" introduce the following clause instead of acting as a parenthetical adverb after "Collier," so it does not correctly punctuate the two intended independent clauses. ✗
  • Choice B — Collier, though;: Produces "...did not originate with Collier, though; in 1985, the idea was first articulated..." — "though" works as a parenthetical adverb modifying the first clause (similar to "admittedly"), and the semicolon after it correctly separates the two independent clauses. ✓
  • Choice C — Collier, though,: Puts commas on both sides of "though," making it a parenthetical, but the two independent clauses are still separated only by commas — still a comma splice. ✗
  • Choice D — Collier; though,: Places the semicolon between "Collier" and "though," which cuts off "though, in 1985..." as the second clause. But "though" at the start of a clause is a subordinating conjunction, making what follows a dependent clause — which cannot stand after a semicolon as if it were independent. ✗
That matches Choice B — Collier, though;.
